title: if and switch

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C++: Beginner to Intermediate draft for if and switch; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: Standard C++ Tour; accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to if and switch
sources.claim: C++ if, switch, and conditional expressions choose paths from contextually converted conditions, with initialization statements able to scope decision state.

canonicalExplanation.summary: C++ if, switch, and conditional expressions choose paths from contextually converted conditions, with initialization statements able to scope decision state.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: An if initializer parses a value and keeps the parse result scoped to the success and failure branches. Your goal is to write and test boundary-complete selection logic. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Program structure and main; pf state expressions; Expressions, operators and conversions. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: C++ if, switch, and conditional expressions choose paths from contextually converted conditions, with initialization statements able to scope decision state. This rule is applied to concrete C++ source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Switch fallthrough, dangling else, scoped initializers, pattern-like variant handling, and floating comparisons require explicit branch coverage. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: Every switch label ends its branch automatically in C++ unless braces are omitted.

includes: Implement complete decisions with scoped variables and clear conditions.
Write and test boundary-complete selection logic.
excludes: Switch fallthrough, dangling else, scoped initializers, pattern-like variant handling, and floating comparisons require explicit branch coverage.

outcomes: Write and test boundary-complete selection logic.

examples.title: if and switch: defined case
examples.situation: An if initializer parses a value and keeps the parse result scoped to the success and failure branches.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: An if initializer parses a value and keeps the parse result scoped to the success and failure branches.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: C++ switch execution falls through following labels until a control transfer; use break or explicit [[fallthrough]] to state intent.
examples.result: The defined result follows because C++ switch execution falls through following labels until a control transfer; use break or explicit [[fallthrough]] to state intent.

examples.title: if and switch: boundary case
examples.situation: A switch uses [[fallthrough]] only where sharing work is intentional and break elsewhere.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: A switch uses [[fallthrough]] only where sharing work is intentional and break elsewhere.
Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Switch fallthrough, dangling else, scoped initializers, pattern-like variant handling, and floating comparisons require explicit branch coverage.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: Every switch label ends its branch automatically in C++ unless braces are omitted.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Write and test boundary-complete selection logic.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Every switch label ends its branch automatically in C++ unless braces are omitted.
misconceptions.correction: C++ switch execution falls through following labels until a control transfer; use break or explicit [[fallthrough]] to state intent.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete CPP rules, explain why this belief fails: Every switch label ends its branch automatically in C++ unless braces are omitted.

analogy.example: A system of self-managing components can model if and switch: each component protects an invariant and owns resources through typed interfaces and lifetime.
usefulFor: Connecting types, value semantics, invariants, generic interfaces, ownership, and deterministic cleanup
limitations: C++ overload resolution, templates, value categories, object lifetime, and undefined behavior have no exact physical equivalent.
Switch fallthrough, dangling else, scoped initializers, pattern-like variant handling, and floating comparisons require explicit branch coverage.
